techniques.evidence_quote: the breach appears to have started when the attackers somehow gained access to the company's Gitlab code repository
a token or credential that gave the bad guys access to Sisense's Amazon S3 buckets in the cloud
in that repository was a token or credential that gave the bad guys access to Sisense's Amazon S3 buckets in the cloud

access tokens are essentially text files on your computer that allow you to stay logged in for extended periods of time — sometimes indefinitely. And depending on which service we're talking about, it may be possible for attackers to re-use those access tokens to authenticate as the victim
the attackers used the S3 access to copy and exfiltrate several terabytes worth of Sisense customer data

the attackers used the S3 access to copy and exfiltrate several terabytes worth of Sisense customer data, which apparently included millions of access tokens, email account passwords, and even SSL certificates

attack_chain.description: Attackers gained unauthorized access to Sisense's self-hosted GitLab code repository, serving as the initial foothold into Sisense's internal development infrastructure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the breach appears to have started when the attackers somehow gained access to the company's Gitlab code repository

attack_chain.description: Within the GitLab repository, attackers discovered hardcoded AWS credentials (tokens or access keys) that were checked into source code, granting access to Sisense's Amazon S3 environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: in that repository was a token or credential that gave the bad guys access to Sisense's Amazon S3 buckets in the cloud

attack_chain.description: Attackers used the stolen AWS credentials/tokens to authenticate to Amazon S3 as a legitimate account, bypassing normal access controls.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a token or credential that gave the bad guys access to Sisense's Amazon S3 buckets in the cloud

attack_chain.description: Using the valid AWS credentials, attackers accessed and copied terabytes of Sisense customer data stored in S3 buckets, including access tokens, email account passwords, and SSL certificates.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attackers used the S3 access to copy and exfiltrate several terabytes worth of Sisense customer data, which apparently included millions of access tokens, email account passwords, and even SSL certificates

attack_chain.description: Attackers exfiltrated several terabytes of collected customer data from Sisense's S3 buckets to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attackers used the S3 access to copy and exfiltrate several terabytes worth of Sisense customer data

attack_chain.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
attack_chain.description: With millions of stolen customer access tokens in hand, attackers gained the capability to authenticate to downstream third-party services as Sisense customers without needing passwords, enabling further downstream compromise.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: it may be possible for attackers to re-use those access tokens to authenticate as the victim without ever having to present valid credentials

initial_access_vector: Attackers gained access to Sisense's self-hosted GitLab repository, where hardcoded AWS credentials were discovered in the source code.
persistence_mechanism: Stolen access tokens allowing long-lived, passwordless re-authentication to downstream customer services
privilege_escalation: AWS S3 token/credential found in GitLab repository provided direct privileged access to cloud storage without requiring additional escalation steps
